facts_disclosed: Would never make pop music — not real art

turns.content: yo so I just had the most ANNOYING conversation with this guy at a label mixer last night and I need to vent
Vent away! What happened at the mixer?

this A&R man comes up to me right, heard one of my sets, says he likes my sound. I'm thinking ok cool maybe something good is happening!! then he hits me with 'have you ever thought about making something more... accessible?' like what does that even MEAN

Ah, the classic "accessible" talk. That's usually industry code for "can you make it more pop-friendly so we can market it easier." It's frustrating because they're essentially asking you to water down what makes your sound yours.

Did he get more specific about what he meant, or was it just that vague suggestion?

oh he got specific still!! told me I should try writing over simpler chord progressions, maybe get a topliner for hooks. basically wants me to strip out everything that makes my ting MINE and turn it into the same recycled pop formula everyone else is doing

Yeah, that's pretty blatant. "Get a topliner for hooks" and "simpler chord progressions" is literally the pop production playbook. Take something unique, sand down the edges, make it radio-friendly.

Here's the thing though — there are labels and A&Rs who specifically want underground and experimental sounds. The fact that this particular guy doesn't get your vision doesn't mean the whole industry won't. What kind of label was he with?

some major sub-label I don't even remember the name. and that's the ting right there — I would NEVER make pop music. like ever. it's not real art to me fam. it's the same 4 chords with the same vocal chain copy pasted across a hundred tracks. where is the SOUL in that??

I hear you. There's a real critique there — a lot of mainstream pop does rely on a pretty narrow set of formulas, and when you're someone who puts genuine creative energy into crafting something different, being told to conform stings.

That said, I want to push back just slightly — not to defend pop, but to make sure you're channeling that energy productively. Knowing exactly what you *won't* do is powerful. It sharpens your brand. What would you say your sound IS, in your own words?

my sound is the UNDERGROUND ting!! dancehall roots, heavy bass music, experimental riddims that make your chest vibrate. I want people to FEEL it not just hear it ahlie. the kind of music that makes you close your eyes and move without thinking. not some algorithmic nonsense designed to get Spotify plays

That's a clear artistic identity right there. Dancehall roots, bass weight, experimental production — that's a lane with a real audience and a growing one too. Think about how bass music and dancehall-influenced sounds have been expanding globally. Artists like Equiknoxx, Murlo, or the whole Night Slugs/Fade to Mind wave proved there's serious appetite for that kind of thing.

The key is finding the right partners — labels, A&Rs, and promoters who are already in that world. Not trying to convince a pop-focused label to understand your vision.

Have you looked at labels that specifically champion that underground dancehall-meets-bass-music space?

yeah I've been looking into a few still!! some UK labels doing mad tings with that sound. and some independent ones here in Toronto too. but it's slow going you know. the underground doesn't have major label money behind it

That's the real trade-off. The underground gives you creative freedom but tighter budgets and slower growth. The upside is that underground audiences are way more loyal — they stick with artists for years, buy merch, show up to every gig.

Some practical moves while you build those label relationships:

- **Bandcamp** is still huge for experimental and bass music. Build a catalog there — even short EPs or singles
- **SoundCloud** remains the discovery platform for underground electronic music
- **Send demos directly** to labels you respect. A cold email with a strong track and a clear description of your sound works more often than people think
- **Collaborate with artists** already on those labels — that's often the fastest way in

The UK labels especially are always looking for fresh perspectives on dancehall and bass music. A Canadian producer with Jamaican roots bringing something different? That's a real pitch.

see THIS is what I needed to hear instead of 'make it more accessible' lol. I've been putting stuff on SoundCloud already and the response from the bass music heads has been wicked. like these people actually LISTEN to the production, they hear the layers

That SoundCloud engagement is actually a great sign. Bass music listeners are notoriously detail-oriented — they pick up on sound design, rhythm patterns, mixing choices. If those people are responding to your work, you're doing something right.

Use that as validation when moments like last night happen. Some A&R at a major telling you to simplify doesn't mean your music isn't connecting — it means it's connecting with the *right* people, just not with him.

Keep building that SoundCloud presence. Engage with the community there — comment on other producers' tracks, repost things you genuinely like. That scene is tight-knit and people notice who's active and authentic.

you're so right. I was in the lab last night after that mixer just FUMING and I made one of the hardest riddims I've ever made lmao. anger is fuel ahlie!!! 808s shaking the walls at 2am my neighbours probably hate me

Ha! Nothing like spite-fueled creativity. Some of the best music comes from that "I'll show them" energy. If that mixer guy could hear what his condescending advice produced, he'd probably rethink his approach.

Just maybe invest in some acoustic treatment or good monitor headphones for the 2am sessions — keeping your neighbours on your side is worth it long-term, especially if you're producing at home regularly.

But seriously, channel that fire. You've got a clear vision, a growing audience that gets it, and the skill to back it up. The right opportunities will come from staying in your lane, not swerving into someone else's.
lol facts about the neighbours!! ok I feel better now honestly. needed to get that out. back to the lab tonight to finish that riddim. appreciate you letting me rant fam!!!

Anytime! Go finish that track and put it on SoundCloud. Let the music speak louder than any A&R's opinion. And next time someone tells you to make pop — just smile, nod, and go make something that shakes walls. Have a good night in the lab!
